coredata.dc:title: Human mesenchymal stem cells encapsulated-coacervated photoluminescent nanodots layered bioactive chitosan/collagen hydrogel matrices to indorse cardiac healing after acute myocardial infarction
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology
coredata.dc:creator: Si, Rui
Gao, Chao
Guo, Rui
Lin, Chen
Li, Jiayi
Guo, Wenyi
coredata.dc:description: Acute Myocardial Infarction (MI) is one of the foremost causes of human death worldwide and it leads to mass death of cardiomyocytes, interchanges of unfavorable biological environment and affecting electrical communications by fibrosis scar formations, and specifically deficiency of blood supply to heart which leads to heart damage and heart failure. Recently, numerous appropriate strategies have been applied to base on solve these problems wound be provide prominent therapeutic potential to cardiac regeneration after acute MI. In the present study, a combined biopolymeric conductive hydrogel was fabricated with conductive ultra-small graphene quantum dots as a soft injectable hydrogel for cardiac regenerations. The resultant hydrogel was combined with human Mesenchymal stem cells (hMSCs) to improved angiogenesis in cardiovascular tissues and decreasing cardiomyocyte necrosis of hydrogel treated acute-infarcted region has been greatly associated with the development of cardiac functions in MI models. The prepared graphene quantum dots and hydrogel groups was physico-chemically analyzed and confirmed the suitability of the materials for cardiac regeneration applications. The in vitro analyzes of hydrogels with hMSCs have established that enhanced cell survival rate, increased expressions of pro-inflammatory factors, pro-angiogenic factors and early cardiogenic markers. The results of in vivo myocardial observations and electrocardiography data demonstrated a favorable outcome of ejection fraction, fibrosis area, vessel density with reduced infarction size, implying that significant development of heart regenerative function after MI. This novel strategy of injectable hydrogel with hMSCs could be appropriate for the effective treatment of cardiac therapies after acute MI.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Myocardial infarction
Graphene
Bioactive hydrogel
Stem cells
Cardiac repair

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CS/CG Hydrogel and CS/CG-GQDs
Isolation and Expansion of Human Mesenchymal Stem Cells (hMSCs)
Preparation of Myocardial Infarction Animal Models and Experimental Groups

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representations of the hydrogel GCC hydrogel formation and the demonstration of the scope of the work.
(a, b) Microscopic observations (TEM), UV/PL spectral data and FTIR spectral analysis of as-prepared GQDs (Inset XRD spectrum of GQDs).
FTIR, TGA spectral and SEM microscopic investigations of prepared different component of hydrogel matrices with GQDs.

In vitro cell compatibility and angiogenic potential of MSCs with prepared hydrogels; (a) In vitro quantitative analysis of MSCs cell proliferation measured by using MTT assay method; (b) Microscopic observation of cell proliferations and tube formations with prepared hydrogel medium after 24 h of incubation; (c) quantitative analysis of area covered (cell migrations) by MSCs in different time intervals (6, 12 and 18 h); (d) Quantitative determinations of tube formation after 24 h.
In vitro early stage cardiac markers expressions treated with as-prepared hydrogels and Fluorescent microscopic observations.
The analysis results fold increase in the in vitro gene expressions of cardiac stress and inflammatory genes were determined by qPCR method.

Echocardiography analysis results of the in vivo cardiac remodeling after injection of the prepared GCC hydrogel with hMSCs implantations at 28-days post treatment injection; (a) Left ventricular internal dimensions of systole and diastole (LVIDs and LVIDd); (b) end systolic and diastolic volume (EVS and EVD) measurement; (c) Fractional shortening; (d) determinations of ejection fraction and fractional area change and Left ventricular posterior wall at systolic and diastolic sites.

In vivo observations of angiogenic potential and reduction of MI site of heart after injections prepared hMSCs-GCC hydrogel. (a) Respective MTS stained cross-sections of infarcted heart after treatment of control, GCC and hMSCs-GCC hydrogel after 28 days post-treatment; (b) Immunohistochemical microscopic images of infarcted regions after treatment with hydrogel samples; (c) collagen content of infarcted regions after hydrogel treatment; (d) measurement of infarcted area reduction; (e) determinations of ratio between heart weight to body weight after treatment and (f) quantitative analysis data of vessels.

head.ce:title: Human mesenchymal stem cells encapsulated-coacervated photoluminescent nanodots layered bioactive chitosan/collagen hydrogel matrices to indorse cardiac healing after acute myocardial infarction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Cardiology, Xijing Hospital, Fourth Military Medical University, Xi'an 710032, Shaanxi, People's Republic of China.
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Cardiology, Xijing Hospital, Fourth Military Medical University, Xi'an, 710032, Shaanxi, People's Republic of China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physiology, Collage of Medicine and Life Sciences, Nanjing University of Chinese Medicine, Nanjing 210023, Jiangsu, People's Republic of China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collage of Medicine and Life Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physiology, Collage of Medicine and Life Sciences, Nanjing University of Chinese Medicine, Nanjing, 210023, Jiangsu, People's Republic of China.

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: NO.127, Changle West Road, Xi'an 710032, Shaanxi, People's Republic of China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Acute Myocardial Infarction (MI) is one of the foremost causes of human death worldwide and it leads to mass death of cardiomyocytes, interchanges of unfavorable biological environment and affecting electrical communications by fibrosis scar formations, and specifically deficiency of blood supply to heart which leads to heart damage and heart failure. Recently, numerous appropriate strategies have been applied to base on solve these problems wound be provide prominent therapeutic potential to cardiac regeneration after acute MI. In the present study, a combined biopolymeric conductive hydrogel was fabricated with conductive ultra-small graphene quantum dots as a soft injectable hydrogel for cardiac regenerations. The resultant hydrogel was combined with human Mesenchymal stem cells (hMSCs) to improved angiogenesis in cardiovascular tissues and decreasing cardiomyocyte necrosis of hydrogel treated acute-infarcted region has been greatly associated with the development of cardiac functions in MI models. The prepared graphene quantum dots and hydrogel groups was physico-chemically analyzed and confirmed the suitability of the materials for cardiac regeneration applications. The in vitro analyzes of hydrogels with hMSCs have established that enhanced cell survival rate, increased expressions of pro-inflammatory factors, pro-angiogenic factors and early cardiogenic markers. The results of in vivo myocardial observations and electrocardiography data demonstrated a favorable outcome of ejection fraction, fibrosis area, vessel density with reduced infarction size, implying that significant development of heart regenerative function after MI. This novel strategy of injectable hydrogel with hMSCs could be appropriate for the effective treatment of cardiac therapies after acute MI.

ce:section.ce:para: Acute Myocardial Infarction (AMI) is one of the serious concerns of cardiovascular diseases which occurs by the lack of prolonged supply of oxygen and nutrients that limits the heart's capacity of anaerobic metabolism in the heart muscles [1,2]. Heart failure, impulsive death and privation of left ventricular function, ischemic heart diseases are the highly complicated exposure arrived by AMI [3–5]. Additionally, disruption of homeostatic balance causes series of successive events like ventricular stiffness, inflammatory effects, necrosis, apoptosis, remodeling that leads to forming scar and altered contractility in heart muscles. Surgical and medical approaches like percutaneous coronary intervention and pharmacotherapy's can significantly enrolls their contribution towards appalling effects of AMI [6–9]. However, they exhibit impotent towards regenerating myocardium and constructing new contractile tissues. Regarding to overcome this problem, experimentally and practically significant risk heart transplantation is only obligatory treatment for AMI. Hence in point of avoiding this complicate treatment, novel recognition of researchers has led to the ideology of cell transplantation, an emerging therapeutic strategy against AMI [10–13].

Cells from various sources of human like cardiac stem cells, bone marrow stem cells, mesenchymal stem cells, hemotoprostic stem cells, embryonic stem cells etc. have exploited for the purpose of regenerative myocardium tissues [14,15]. Even though cell therapies made by different forms of cells have been favorable to improve the function of cardiac, they are not the real accepted occupier of heart tissues. Additionally, studies on stem cells apart from cardiac stem cells reported certain disadvantages like inconsistent efficacy, no effect in reducing scar size and shrinkage of scar tissue. Cardiac stem cells have persuasive activities like formation of smooth muscles cells, endothelial and angiogenic cells. Furthermore, cardiac stem cells are characteristic of distinguishable towards immunomodulatory and immunosuppressive properties [16–19].

As well, a promising strategy and biocompatible, biodegradable materials are required for efficient cardiac stem cells transplantation to associate the treatment for AMI. Hence a pouch like hydrogel materials like collagen, gelatin, and fibroin can serve as a potential and medically advantageous candidate in drug delivery systems, cell therapy, creation and regeneration of muscle tissues and controlled release of systems [17,20,21]. Nowadays, hydrogel materials are widely used in biomedical and pharmaceutical field for its distinct properties like biocompatible, biodegradable, swelling ability and non-toxicity [22–24]. Combination of biopolymers like chitosan, with hydrogel has been demonstrated as an efficient candidate for stem cell transplantation. Since chitosan is a trademark example for its excellent biocompatible, biodegradable, antimicrobial, immunostimulatory activities [25,26]. Materials fabricated with chitosan have been widely reported and investigated in medical field such as drug release, tissue engineering, catalysts and enzyme carrier, medical implantation, wound healing and also in other fields as recovery of metal ions, energy storage, protective clothing materials, biosensors and energy storage [27–29]. In recent years, bioactive quantum dots have steadily increasing attentions in different bio-medical fields such as drug delivery, tissue engineering, bioimaging and biosensors, due to its potential properties of excellent mechanical abilities, biocompatibility, transparency and electrical conductivity. Generally, electro-active nanoparticles (e.g., Au, carbon, etc.,) have been played a greatly in the cardiac regeneration and myocardial infarction to enhance electrical communication between the adjacent cardiac cells. In the present investigation, to increasing nanoparticles potential we have used the graphene quantum dots [30–35].

Furthermore, incorporation of graphene oxide quantum dots (GQDs) can bring a surprised increasing change in the ability of stem cells encapsulated on polymeric hydrogel materials due to its amazing properties like high surface area, optical properties, stable fluorescence, dispersible in water, photostability, low cytotoxicity, autocatalytic, antioxidant, antimicrobial, biocompatible and biodegradable nature [36–40]. Significant utilization of GQDs in biomedicine as efficient drug carrier, photothermal agents, tissue engineering, bioimaging etc. is its progressive growth [41,42]. In the present investigation, we have designed a novel formulation of blended bio-polymeric hydrogel with graphene quantum dots as cell carriers for the cardiac regeneration after myocardial infarction. The influence of various factors of bio-hydrogel and stem cells were investigated physico-chemical and biological methods for the suitability of cardiac repair.

The graphene precursor (GO), Chitosan was obtained from Alfa Aesar company. Type I collagen, biological mediums (DMEM), anti-biotics (penicillin, streptomycin) and other biological reagents were purchased from Invitrogen (Carlsbad, CA). And other chemicals such as potassium permanganate, dimethyl formamide, sodium nitrate, hydrogen peroxide were obtained as analytical grade with high purity (>98) from Shanghai Chemical company, China. All the chemicals and reagents were used in the reaction without any further purification processes.

Preparation of GQDs was initiated by forming its precursor, graphene oxide by its approved modified Hummer's method [43,44]. Followed by the preparation of graphene oxide, solvothermal method is applied to prepare GQDs using dimethyl formamide as solvent. In a typical procedure, about 200 mg of graphene oxide were mixed with 20 mL of dimethyl formamide. Reaction mixture was subjected to ultrasonic dispersion to arrive homogeneously dispersed solution and subjected to microwave radiation for about 1 h at 30 °C. Radiated reaction mixture was heated in muffle furnace for about 10 h at 100 °C. To the obtained precursor of graphene quantum dots, add 100 mg of coal tar pitch suspended in 100 mL of hydrogen peroxide and carried out the ultrasonication process for about 1 h followed by refluxing for 2 h at 100 °C. The color of the reaction mixture changed from brown to deep yellow that indicates the formation of GQDs. After cooling, the solution was then filtered using 0.22 μm pore sized filter paper to remove insoluble fragments of coal tar pitch. Then the filtrate was dried by vacuum freeze drying to obtain pure GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CS/CG Hydrogel and CS/CG-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare CS/CG hydrogel, at first, chitosan was obtained by the deacetylation processing of chitin. Initially 5% solution of collagen was prepared with acetic acid (100 mL) and subjected to stirring at 50 °C and 500 rpm to give homogeneous solution of collagen. About 10 mg/mL of chitosan dissolved in acetic acid was added into the collagen solution followed by immersing this reaction mixture in temperature-controlled water bath with simultaneous magnetic stirring at 60 °C for about 3 h under nitrogen gas atmosphere. The pH of the reaction mixture should be maintained in between 7 and 8 at basic condition using 1 M NaOH solution. The formed hydrogel was cooled to 5 °C, lyophilized and dried in a freeze dryer for 12 h to acquire CS/CG hydrogel. In view of preparing CS/CG-GQDs, about 0.5 g of GQDs were suspended in 25 mL of water and 0.5 g of CS/CG hydrogel and 10 mL of 1% citric acid solution was slowly added at constant stirring at 100 °C. This reaction mixture was subjected for continuous stirring of about 6 h. After the characteristic time of stirring, the formed CS/CG-GQDs was separated using filtration using Whatmann filter paper and dried in hot air oven at 80 °C for about 1 h. The prepared CS/CG-GQDs should be stored at 20 °C for further use.

The suitability of the prepared hydrogel samples for the cardiac applications was examined via in vitro cell survival and cell migration analysis by using human coronary artery endothelial cells (HCAECs). Before cell seeding, the hydrogel samples were washed by using culture medium (DMEM and FBS media) with anti-biotics (penicillin-streptomycin (1%)). Then, the collected HCAEC cells were cultured on the surface of hydrogel with freshly prepared Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) supplemented with fetal bovine serum (FBS; 10%), penicillin (100 UmL−1) and streptomycin (100 UmL−1) at appropriate humidified incubating conditions (37 °C; 5% CO2). The cell survival rate and cell migration percentage were observed by MTT assay kit and Image J software, respectively. And the live/dead assay was visualized by CLSM fluorescence microscopic assay by using FDA and PI dual staining's.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Isolation and Expansion of Human Mesenchymal Stem Cells (hMSCs)
ce:section.ce:para: Human mesenchymal stem cells (hMSCs) were isolated from female Wister rats weighing about 250 g with 20–23 weeks of age. Initially the atrial samples of rats were collected and cut into small pieces followed by suspension in F12 K medium ref. which also contains collagenase [1–3 mg/mL). Then the cells were plated in petridishes with excess of F12 medium mixed with 10% fetal bovine serum, recombinant human fibroblast (100 mg/mL), l-glutathione in minimal amounts and human erythropoietin (10 μg/mL). After 8–10 days of incubation this cultured plates, cells maintained and expanded mostly c-kit CSCs. Expanded cells were allowed for immunomagnetic separation with microbeads to obtain c-kit CSCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Myocardial Infarction Animal Models and Experimental Groups
ce:section.ce:para: Induction of myocardial infarction was performed to the procedure according to the authorized literature procedure. Care and animal experimental procedure were followed according to the guidelines provided by the approved Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee, China. Ten Male Fischer rats with the age of about 5–7 weeks and weighing about 250–300 g were acquired from Chinese Medical University and housed 3 per cage, maintained at room temperature and atmospheric conditions. Ad libitum food and water were provided for the rats and experiment was started after animals become comfortable for the new environment. Separation of animals groups into 4 groups have carried out randomly with each group carries 4 animals. All the groups were subjected for the induction of MI as reported in literature. Group I served as the model without injection of prepared samples. One week later after the induction of MI, other groups were injected accordingly by the prepared samples. Group II was injected by GQDs (0.5 μg/μL) with saline into the infracted area with a 30 gauge needle syringe. Group III was treated with CS/CG-GQDs, Group IV was injected with CSCs alone (2 × 105 cells in 100 μL saline and Group IV was introduced by the injection of CSCs encapsulated CS/CG-GQDs to find out the efficiency of the prepared samples against myocardial infarction. Identification of the prepared samples for the successful injection in the infracted area was found by the bleb over the infarct zone. Rats were monitored in a climate-controlled room and allowed freely to access food and water.

Structural moieties of GQDs, CS/CG hydrogel, CS/CG-GQDs were studied by FT-IR analysis. Further nature of crystallinity, grain size parameters of GQDs, CS/CG hydrogel, CS/CG-GQDs were evaluated by employing XRD technique. Cu-Kα radiation with wavelength of 0.154 nm, scanning rate of 0.02°/s in the 2θ range, 40 kV accelerating voltage and 40 mA current was applied to record XRD patterns. To study about exact morphological structure and size, transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL-2100F) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL-5600LV) techniques were adopted. TEM specimens were prepared by drop-casting the as-prepared sample dispersions onto carbon-coated TEM grids and dried in air. The thermal stability and phase degradation of hydrogel components were examined by the thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA, EXSTAR 6000, Japan). The morphology of the cells with hydrogel samples were visualized by laser scanning confocal microscopic (Leica TCS SP2) technique after in vitro analyses.

In recent days, the fabrications of hydrogels with fluorescent nanomaterials were investigated and applied in the biomedical applications due to its effective sensitivity of targeted therapeutic potential, especially the hydrogels containing fluorescent carbon-based quantum dots [45, 46]. The schematic representation of the present investigation is exhibited in Fig. 1 to understand the main aim of the research study. Hence, the incorporation of GQDs into the bio-composited hydrogels provide an active physiological and biological properties with stable fluorescent system. Firstly, the spherical shape and ultra-small size of the synthesized GQDs were confirmed by the TEM micrographs and DLS analyses. The resulting analyses (Fig. 2a) of the GQDs exhibit the particle size about the 4–10 nm, which was further confirmed by the DLS measurement. The absorption/photoluminescent spectra and FTIR spectral analysis results of the GQDs were confirmed the successful formation of GQDs as exhibited in Fig. 2c and d. In addition, the formation of GQDs was proved by the XRD analysis (inserted), which displays wide peak around 26 (2θ) corresponded to the (002) reflections.

Fig. 3(a) exhibits the resulting FTIR analysis of the prepared GQDs and GQDs loaded hydrogel materials to understand the interactions between the bio-composited hydrogel. The spectrum of GQDs displays absorption stretching vibrations at lower and higher wavenumbers at the ranges of 1350 cm−1 and 2950 cm−1, confirming the successful formation of graphene quantum dots by the used synthesis protocols. Additionally, the characteristics peaks around 1450 to 1625 cm−1 and 3000 to 3100 cm−1 corresponded to the skeletal and stretching vibrations of aromatic rings, which clearly demonstrated that the formations graphene network. The spectrum of chitosan and GQDs loaded hydrogel displays a wide absorption band at the range of 3150 cm−1 corresponded to the hydroxyl (OH) and NH functional groups at the edges. In addition, the peaks of CO (stretching), CC (stretching) and CN bonds have assigned at the ranges of 1710 cm−1, 1665 cm−1, 1566 cm−1 and 1395 cm−1, respectively. The slight disappearance of peak at 1720 cm−1 was confirmed the interaction between carboxyl group of GQDs and amine groups of chitosan hydrogel matrix. The spectral data of GQDs loaded hydrogel was more similar to the bonds of GQDs and bare components (chitosan and collagen) of hydrogels, which could be a reasonable confirmation of the effective interactions between GQDs and bio-polymeric hydrogel networks. The thermal gravimetric (TGA) analysis of the bare collagen, CS/CG hydrogel and GQDs loaded hydrogel demonstrated the successful interactions CS and GQDs and improved thermal stability as shown in Fig. 3(b).

The hydrogel scaffolds for the biological applications should have an applicable morphological structure with greater surface area and the capability to provide transportations of nutrients and biological factors to influence the favorable cellular mechanisms and dynamics (Fig. 3c, d &e). In the present investigations, the prepared hydrogel groups have a 3D porous network, which is highly favorable for the cardiac healing applications. The prepared GQDs incorporated CS/CG porous hydrogel network significantly permits an appropriate permeability of micro- and macro natured biological molecules and nutrients which could be provide an ideal microenvironment for cellular colonization and vascularization among cells embedded living tissues of cardiac repair. Fig. 3(c & d) exhibits the SEM microscopic structure of the CS/CG hydrogel and GQDs loaded hydrogel structure, which revealed the excellent and uniform porous nature. The micrographic results demonstrated that the average pores size of the CS/CG and GQDs-CS/CG hydrogels was 20 ± 3 μm and 32 ± 5 and, respectively. The microscopic observations imply that the addition of collagen molecules into the CS network has led to a favorable interconnected porous structure and also influenced into the pores size of the hydrogel, due to self-assembled fibrillogenesis process of collagen molecules. The two stages of process have been involved in the formation of collagen hydrogel with CS molecules such as lag phase and growth phase. The collagen molecules forms dimers/trimers aggregate and produces subfibrillar units with support of stabilizing aldehyde components of telopeptides, which can linearly and crosswise interact with other similar collagen molecules to form uniform and interconnected porous structured hydrogel network. The GQDs loaded hydrogels were also investigated and visualized by TEM analysis (Fig. 3e). The TEM micrograph results established that ultra-small quantum dots were uniformly distributed in the hydrogel network without any accumulations, while no QDs were exhibited in the bare hydrogel sample.

The biocompatibility and suitability of the fabricated hydrogel matrixes were examined via in vitro methods. The in vitro cell viability and cell proliferations were investigated by using human coronary artery endothelial cells (HCAECs) cultured on the prepared GCC hydrogels and the values were quantitatively measured as exhibited in Fig. 4 . Its well-known that chitosan and collagen-based hydrogels have significantly favorable for the cell proliferations. These presented results demonstrated that GQDs loaded CS/CG hydrogel reliably had significant survival rate and proliferation percentage compared to the bare CS/CG hydrogel matrices. The cell proliferations have no significant effect on 6 h and 12 h for the hydrogels. Nonetheless, on 18 h, the proliferation percentages have increased significantly on the GCC hydrogel sample. In addition, we have investigated cardiomyocytes proliferation ability of the hydrogels were preliminary investigated by using early cardiac transcription factors with cardiomyocyte progenitor (hCMPs) cells. The cultured samples of GCC hydrogels have a significant enhancement in the selected early cardiac markers (SAC, Cx43 and cTnl) after 5 days of culture. These results suggest that GCC hydrogel have favorable cardiac progenitors and cardiomyocytes mitotic activity as exhibited in Fig. 5 . Hence, we could be preliminary confirm the prepared hydrogel can be suitable for in vivo proliferation and differentiation of cardiomyocytes in cardiac regeneration.

The hemodynamic examinations of saline and hydrogel treated groups were measured and exhibited in Table 1. No significant body weight and heart rate differences were observed between the hydrogel groups in the presence and absence of hMSCs after 4-weeks post treatment. The hMSCs-GCC hydrogel group and control sample have enhanced cardiac output, contractility index and LV pressure when associated to the GCC hydrogel and saline treated groups. And the preliminary in vivo cytocompatibility of the hydrogel samples were examined to confirm no significant immune response against model animals. The resulting studies of Immuno-histochemical staining on myocardial tissues demonstrated that there is no significant damages of cardiomyocytes and inflammation factors (TNF-α) after hMSCs-GCC and GCC hydrogels injection as shown in Fig. 6 . In addition, there was no significant differences of pro-inflammatory factors (TNFα & miR-146) and cardiomyocyte apoptotic gene (miR-145 & Cyclin D1) and miRNA expressions in hydrogel samples when compared to the control (Fig. 6). Therefore, we could be concluded that the prepared hydrogel samples are greatly compatible for the cardiac regeneration applications.

In order to examine the action of hMSCs encapsulated GCC bio-hydrogels on the post myocardial infarction cardiac functions after intramyocardial injection to the model animal myocardial infarction models. The cardiac function of infracted model before and after hMSCs-GCC hydrogels and control were evaluated by the echocardiographic techniques as exhibited in Fig. 7 . The results of echocardiography were established that hMSCs encapsulated GCC bio-hydrogels injected MI group have significantly reduced the LV remodeling after 30-post treatment, which is very important to avert heart failures through myocardial infarction. The ejection fraction (EF; 78.56 ± 5.45) and fractional area change (FA; 58.34 ± 3.5) were substantial improvement in the hCPCs encapsulated hydrogels when compared to the GCC hydrogel and saline group. In mean time, GCC hydrogel group also have reasonable improvement of EF and FA change, due to its active functional groups structure and biocompatible activity. The left ventricle internal dimensions of hydrogel treated group during diastole (LVId) and systole (LVIs) were 6.43 ± 0.50 mm and 4.45 ± 0.25 mm, respectively, which is improved values when compared to the GCC hydrogel and saline treated group. And hCPCs-GCC hydrogel treated group also have improved values of end-systolic (ES) and end-diastolic (ED) volumes such as 0.22 ± 0.05 and 0.59 ± 0.04 mL, respectively, confirming the operative cardiac functioning of hydrogel samples. The wall measurement of left ventricular posterior at systole and diastole have improved greatly after hydrogels treated groups in the presence and absence of hCPCs compared to the saline treated groups. From those observations, we can be confirming that hCPCs encapsulated hydrogel sample have greater cardiac performances as well as GCC hydrogel without hCPCs also provide a reasonable cardiac function, due to its biocompatibility and presence of chemically active groups.

As published literatures, vascular repair mechanisms can be establishing the prominent recovery and regeneration of myocardial injury, therefore, these are highly essential for the development of the heart function after acute myocardial infarction. In order to examine the therapeutic effect and angiogenesis indorsement of the prepared hMSCs-GCC hydrogels into the injected infarcted heart of an animal model, the myocardial was separated after animal sacrifice and immune-stained within the peri-infarct region as exhibited in Fig. 8(a). These stained microscopic results have exhibited that significant enhancement of positive cells at larger density in the hMSCs-GCC hydrogels treated animals when compared to the GCC hydrogel and control group (Fig. 8b). The microscopic observations were not only exhibit the capillary formation with hMSCs-GCC hydrogel but also establish the existence of highly matured vessels by the immune-staining. And these in vivo analyses have demonstrated that the existence of pro-angiogenic growth factors and favorable cytokines within the hMSCs-hydrogel treated samples, confirms the role of hMSCs to promote angiogenesis into the infarcted heart. As shown in Fig. 8 a and c, histological observations result of hydrogels treated infarcted heart demonstrated that the ability to reduce the extending activity of cardiac remodeling and collagen content, which is happened by cardiomyocytes necrosis factors. These results established that infarct size and infarct area of the hCPCs-GCC hydrogel have been greatly reduced compared to the GCC hydrogel and saline treated groups. The infarct area and infarct size of the treated groups were semi-quantitatively analyzed by the cross-sections of mid papillary muscle after 4-weeks post treatment. Consequently, the higher cardiomyocytes survival in the peri-infarct segment was exhibited in the hCPCs-GCC hydrogel and control treated groups ensuing improved wall thickness compared to the saline treated group. Consequently, the significant increasing angiogenesis and decreasing cardiomyocyte necrosis of hydrogel treated acute-infarcted region has been greatly associated with the development of cardiac functions in MI models.

Recent researchers have established that stem cell therapy have prominent effects on MI treatment, but still has some difficulties to deliver the stem cells at infarcted site. The injection of biocompatible hydrogel system into the myocardial infarcted site could provide effective benefits of mechanical stabilization, delivering biomolecule and stem cells that reduce the extension of pathological remodeling. For that, injected hydrogel system should be having suitable biodegradability, biocompatibility, injectability, presence of functional groups to carrying biomolecules and mechanical abilities. To achieve the above requirements, we have developed the blended chitosan/collagen hydrogel system with suitable component level. Chitosan is a naturally occurring biocompatible polysaccharide, which has no toxic degradation products and non-immunogenic. In addition, chitosan molecules have bundles of amino functional groups on the polysaccharide backbone, which allows conjugation with numerous biomolecules. These functional groups could also play an efficient role in enhancing the cell transplantation efficiency [12,47,48]. From the reported literatures, Collagen I is demonstrated a natural bio-polymer and also a major component of the cardiac ECM. Generally, collagen-based materials have been used in the in vitro cell cultures, and applied to hydrogel backbone formation of in vivo applications. Collagen polymer can be form reconstructed bio-hydrogel materials that highly similar to the moveable connective tissues, which has greater ability to attach many stem cells types with lower inflammatory response [49–51]. Therefore, we have developed the blended formation chitosan-collagen bio-hydrogel for in vitro implantation of stem cells and investigated its suitability for use in vivo cardiac regeneration. In recent days, the only therapeutic choice of end-stage cardiovascular and heart failures is full heart transplantation. But it has high difficulties for organ transplantations due to its lack of donors and matching donors of patients. Hence, the researchers tried to develop the alternative therapeutic route such as injection of cells biomolecules and biomaterials into the infarcted sites. The delivery of stem various cell types, including mesenchymal stem cells, hematopoietic stem cells, skeletal myoblast and cardiac progenitor cells have been used to treat MI and heart failures. Particularly, mesenchymal stem cells implantation has greatest positive outcome because its significantly differentiated into cardiomyocytes, adipocytes, endothelial cells and smooth muscle cells under precise conditions [52–54]. Stem cells implantation into infarcted site could improve myocardial regeneration, significantly reduce infarct size and enhance cardiac function. The present report revealed that hMSCs implanted hydrogel samples greatly reduce infarcted size and post-infarction LV remodeling, enhance in left ventricle ejection fraction and therapeutic outcomes. These resulted data imply that hMSCs implanted hydrogels could be have substantial therapeutic potential in the treatment of cardiac repair after MI. The presented results demonstrated that chitosan mixed collagen hydrogel has provided better stem cells survival and cardiac repair after myocardial infarction by using rat model. The present investigation demonstrated that the nano-combined hydrogels with stem cells have significant effect on cardiac regenerative functions.

In summary, the facile fabricated injectable hydrogel encapsulated with hMSCs was employed greatly to the cardiac remodeling after acute MI. The physico-chemical analysis and morphological results have established the chemical interactions of polymeric molecules with graphene materials, which was favorable for the biological implantations. The in vitro investigations data demonstrated a biocompatibility and suitability of the hydrogels with early cardiac markers. The hMSCs encapsulated hydrogel was successfully injected into the myocardial infarcted site of animal models, attaining a prominent activity for the cardiac regeneration with enhanced activities of vessel density, ejection fraction with reduced infarcted size of the MI heart site. The investigative results imply a novel design of cardiac implant materials and new avenue of cardiac remodeling therapeutic approach after acute MI.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Human induced pluripotent stem cell-derived cardiomyocyte encapsulating bioactive hydrogels improve rat heart function post myocardial infarction
Stem Cell Rep.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Chow, D.J. Stuckey, E. Kidher, M. Rocco, R.J. Jabbour, C.A. Mansfield, A. Darzi, S.E. Harding, M.M. Stevens, T. Athanasiou, Human Induced Pluripotent Stem Cell-Derived Cardiomyocyte Encapsulating Bioactive Hydrogels Improve Rat Heart Function Post Myocardial Infarction, Stem Cell Reports. 9 (2017) 1415–1422. doi:10.1016/j.stemcr.2017.09.003.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The role of tissue engineering in cellular therapies for myocardial infarction: a review
J. Mater. Chem. B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.K. Wu, J. Yu, The role of tissue engineering in cellular therapies for myocardial infarction: A review, Journal of Materials Chemistry B. 3 (2015) 6401–6410. doi:10.1039/c5tb00739a.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanoparticle based delivery of hypoxia-regulated VEGF transgene system combined with myoblast engraftment for myocardial repair
Biomaterials.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Ye, W. Zhang, L.P. Su, H.K. Haider, K.K. Poh, M.J. Galupo, G. Songco, R.W. Ge, H.C. Tan, E.K.W. Sim, Nanoparticle based delivery of hypoxia-regulated VEGF transgene system combined with myoblast engraftment for myocardial repair, Biomaterials. 32 (2011) 2424–2431. doi:10.1016/j.biomaterials.2010.12.008.
